logo.description: Grey AI logo — icon/symbol + text combo, black and grey colors

safe_zone.description: Gemini must leave this area EMPTY — no text, no visual elements, no gradients that obscure this zone

logo.overlay_instructions: After downloading the Gemini image, overlay the logo PNG at top-right (x=820, y=20) with max width 200px. Use Canva, Figma, or the provided Python script.

diagram_label.note: Used for node labels and annotations in concept diagrams

visual_style.philosophy: Editorial. Bold. Emotional. The image IS the argument — not decoration for it.
visual_style.grain_texture: Subtle film grain on ALL backgrounds. Never flat.
visual_style.negative_space: Min 40% of image should be breathing room
visual_style.concept_made_visible: The highest-performing images don't decorate the caption — they EXPLAIN it. The viewer should understand the core idea from the image alone, before reading a single word of the caption.
never_do: Stock photo people smiling at laptops
Generic AI imagery (glowing brains, robots, matrix code, circuit boards)
More than 12 words total on any concept_diagram image
More than 8 words on any other single image
Paragraphs or bullet points on the image
Colorful rounded rectangles with emoji icons (the LinkedIn clip-art trap)
ANY visual elements in the top-right corner (reserved for logo)

dark_gradient.description: Deep charcoal to black gradient with subtle grain texture

cyan_wash.description: Soft cyan-to-white gradient with light grain

dark_diagram.description: Near-black with very subtle grid or dot-matrix texture at low opacity — like technical graph paper in the dark. Gives diagram elements a surface to sit on.
dark_diagram.texture: Faint 40px grid lines or dot grid at 4% opacity — barely visible, structural

concept_diagram.best_for: Process flows, comparisons, transformations, frameworks, cause-and-effect — any post where the IDEA can be drawn
concept_diagram.philosophy: Think editorial infographic meets architectural drawing. NOT a PowerPoint slide. The diagram uses geometry, arrows, and labels to make the concept legible at a glance. The dark background and cyan accent make it feel premium, not corporate.

diagram_elements.nodes: Simple geometric shapes only — rectangles with sharp corners, circles, or raw text in boxes. Dark fill (#1A1A1A or #000) with white or cyan border (1-2px). No gradients on nodes. No shadows. No rounded corners.
diagram_elements.arrows: Clean single-line arrows. Straight or 90-degree only — no curves. White (#FFF) at 60% opacity for neutral flow, cyan (#ADFBF6) at 100% for the key transformation or insight arrow.
diagram_elements.labels: DM Sans Bold, 22-32pt. White for neutral labels. Cyan for the insight or outcome label. ALL CAPS for node labels, sentence case for annotations.
diagram_elements.annotations: Small secondary text (16-20pt, #9A9A9A) for sub-labels or clarifying context. Used sparingly — max 2 annotations per image.
diagram_elements.accent_element: One element only gets cyan highlight — the transformation, the solution, the key insight. Everything else stays white/grey. The eye goes straight to cyan.

composition_rules: Left-to-right or top-to-bottom flow only — Western reading direction
Max 4 nodes per diagram — complexity kills the point
The concept should be readable in under 3 seconds
Leave at least 120px margin on all sides
One dominant headline above or below the diagram — states what the diagram proves
Diagram sits in center 60% of the image — surrounded by breathing room
concept_diagram.reference_aesthetic: Think: a diagram from a premium research report or a Pentagram-designed annual report. Dark, minimal, typographically precise. Not a whiteboard photo. Not a Canva template.

diagram_concept_triggers.description: When analyzing a post draft, use concept_diagram when the post contains ANY of these patterns
triggers: A process with distinct steps (do X, then Y, then Z)
A gap between two states (where people are vs where they should be)
diagram_concept_triggers.note: When a concept_diagram is appropriate, pitch it as one of the 3 options — but make it meaningfully different from a split_contrast. The diagram actually DRAWS the concept. The split_contrast just contrasts two text states.
